title: How banks have adjusted their lending in response to trade tensions
gist: Analysing AnaCredit loan data and the euro area bank lending survey, the ECB finds that euro area banks most exposed to firms exporting to the United States cut loan supply and tightened credit standards as US tariffs and trade tensions escalated in 2025, while trade uncertainty simultaneously dampened firms' loan demand.

themes.theme: Uneven bank exposure to US trade risk

insights.claim: Euro area banks are more exposed to US export-related credit risks than to import-related risks, because their corporate borrowers rely more heavily on selling into the United States than on inputs sourced from it.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Screen euro area bank credit portfolios by borrower export dependence on the US, not import dependence, when assessing tariff-driven loan-loss risk.
insights.quote: euro area banks are more exposed to US export-related risks than to import-related risks
insights.locator: First, euro area banks are more exposed

insights.claim: Export-related trade risk is highly concentrated: a small number of banks sit in a long right tail of very high exposure to euro area exports to the United States, while most banks are only moderately exposed.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Idiosyncratic tail risk means a handful of banks could see outsized loan-book stress from US tariffs even if the aggregate system looks resilient.
insights.quote: a small number of banks are highly exposed to trade risks stemming from euro area exports to the United States, as shown by the long right tail of the blue distribution
insights.locator: Third, a small number of banks

insights.claim: Import-related trade risk is concentrated in the low-exposure range, so most banks face limited credit risk from their borrowers' reliance on US imports.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Import-channel tariff effects are a lower priority than export-channel effects when stress-testing euro area bank books.
insights.locator: By contrast, import-related risks are concentrated

insights.claim: The ECB measures bank-level trade exposure using value added rather than gross flows and the Leontief inverse of the global input-output matrix to capture indirect trade linkages transmitted through intermediate inputs across sectors and countries.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Exposure metrics that ignore higher-order supply-chain linkages will understate true tariff transmission into bank portfolios.
insights.locator: To obtain euro area banks' exposure to trade risk

insights.claim: About half of the banks participating in the euro area bank lending survey reported in 2025 that trade risks were important and expected similar exposure in 2026, across a survey of 153 banks in the January 2026 round.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Trade risk is now a mainstream, persistent concern for roughly half the euro area banking system rather than a niche exposure.
insights.quote: about half of the banks participating in the euro area BLS reported that trade risks were important, and expected similar levels of exposure in 2026
insights.locator: In 2025, about half of the banks

themes.theme: Tighter credit supply from trade-exposed banks

insights.claim: Banks most exposed to borrowers exporting to the United States cut their loan supply the most from April 2025, coinciding with the escalation of tariff threats and trade tensions.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Tariff shocks propagate into a real credit-supply contraction concentrated at the most trade-exposed lenders, tightening financing for exporters.
insights.quote: the banks most exposed to borrowers exporting to the United States have reduced their loan supply the most since April 2025
insights.locator: Chart 2 suggests that the banks most exposed

insights.claim: The dampening effect on loan supply was most pronounced between April and October 2025 at the peak of US-EU trade disputes, then moderated as sentiment improved following the preliminary US-EU trade framework agreement negotiated over the summer.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Credit tightening tracks trade-policy uncertainty closely, so de-escalation and framework deals can quickly ease the supply drag.
insights.locator: The dampening impact on loan supply

insights.claim: A net 11% of banks reported stricter credit standards in 2025 owing to changes in global trade policies and associated uncertainty, with similar effects expected in 2026.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The quantified net tightening gives a concrete gauge of how much trade policy is already crimping euro area corporate credit conditions.
insights.quote: A net 11% of banks reported stricter credit standards in 2025 owing to changes in global trade policies and the associated uncertainty
insights.locator: A net 11% of banks reported stricter

insights.claim: The tightening was driven by lower risk tolerance and concerns over credit quality, reflecting heightened prudence despite banks' overall solid balance sheets.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The pullback is a risk-appetite decision rather than a capital constraint, so it can reverse faster than a balance-sheet-driven credit crunch.
insights.quote: These decisions were driven by lower risk tolerance and concerns over credit quality, reflecting heightened prudence despite banks' solid balance sheets overall
insights.locator: These decisions were driven by lower risk tolerance

insights.claim: The ECB isolates loan-supply shocks from firm demand shocks using the Amiti and Weinstein (2018) methodology applied to granular bank-firm AnaCredit data.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Separating supply from demand matters because falling loan volumes could otherwise be misread as pure demand weakness rather than deliberate bank retrenchment.
insights.locator: Loan supply shocks are extracted using

insights.claim: Trade tensions dampened firms' loan demand, with a net -6% of banks reporting a decrease in 2025 and a net -3% expecting the decline to continue in 2026.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Trade uncertainty hits credit from both sides, so weaker euro area loan growth reflects suppressed investment demand, not just tighter supply.
insights.quote: a net -6% of banks reported a decrease in 2025, while a net -3% expected this decline to continue in 2026

insights.claim: Economic policy uncertainty has weakened credit dynamics, and recent trade tensions have exacerbated this by reducing loan demand alongside tighter supply.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Watch policy-uncertainty gauges as a leading signal for euro area corporate credit and investment weakness.
insights.quote: Overall, economic policy uncertainty has weakened credit dynamics.

insights.claim: In some sectors such as the car industry, trade-related tightening compounded pre-existing structural vulnerabilities.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Autos are a stacked-risk sector where tariff stress layers onto structural weakness, meriting closer credit scrutiny than trade exposure alone implies.
insights.quote: In some cases (e.g. in the car industry) trade-related tightening compounded pre-existing structural vulnerabilities.
insights.locator: In some cases (e.g. in the car industry)

insights.claim: Banks responded to trade risk primarily by stepping up monitoring of exposed borrowers, with some choosing to watch closely without changing credit standards while others tightened, especially for firms in highly trade-exposed sectors.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Intensified monitoring is an early-warning behaviour that can precede broader standard tightening if trade conditions deteriorate.
insights.locator: Since mid-2025, several banks have reported

insights.claim: Adjustments were more pronounced among banks most heavily exposed to trade risks, particularly where those risks compounded existing corporate vulnerabilities.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The credit-tightening response is nonlinear, concentrating where trade and structural risks overlap rather than spreading evenly.

themes.theme: Credit standards as a leading indicator

insights.claim: Shifts in credit standards have historically preceded changes in annual firm loan growth by about a year across the euro area.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The 2025 net tightening in credit standards signals a likely slowdown in euro area firm loan growth through roughly 2026.
insights.quote: Shifts in credit standards have historically preceded changes in annual firm loan growth by about a year across the euro area
